summary:
The Nazca Lines (created c. 200 BC–700 AD) are enormous geoglyphs — lines, geometric figures, and animal and plant designs — etched into the coastal desert plateau of southern Peru by the Nazca culture, and represent one of the most enigmatic archaeological phenomena in the world. Created by removing the reddish-brown iron oxide-coated pebbles on the surface to reveal the yellowish-grey ground beneath, the lines include hundreds of straight lines (some extending for kilometers), dozens of geometric figures, and approximately 70 animal and plant designs including a hummingbird, condor, spider, monkey, and killer whale — most ranging from 100–300 meters in size and visible only from the air.

The sheer scale — covering over 450 square kilometers — and the precision of the geometric lines (which remain straight across uneven terrain) have fueled centuries of speculation, including fringe theories about alien construction. Archaeological evidence clearly demonstrates that the Nazca people created them using simple wooden stakes and cord, and the consistency of the lines reflects careful pre-planning rather than supernatural assistance. The most widely accepted scholarly interpretations link the lines to water and fertility rituals in a region of extreme aridity — astronomical alignments, ritual walking paths, and offerings to gods controlling water and agriculture. The lines were designated a UNESCO World Heritage Site in 1994. Their remarkable preservation is due to the exceptional dryness and stillness of the Nazca plateau.
